title: Psalms 69
subtitle: Save Me, O God, for the Waters Have Come Up to My Neck

sections.header: Verses 1’18 — “I Am Sinking in the Miry Depths”

heb.paragraph: "Those who hate me (shiṭnâ) without reason outnumber the hairs of my head" (v.4). The second most-quoted messianic psalm after Psalm 22. The waters engulf David (v.1), he sinks in mire (v.2), his throat is parched (v.3). "Zeal for your house consumes me, and the insults of those who insult you fall on me" (v.9) — applied to Jesus cleansing the temple (John 2:17). David is mocked: sackcloth becomes a byword, drunkards make songs about him (vv.11–12).

notes.note: "Save me, O God, for the waters have come up to my neck. I sink in the miry depths. My throat is parched." Total submersion: neck-deep water, bottomless mud, dry throat. The imagery is of drowning in slow motion.
"Those who hate me without reason outnumber the hairs of my head." Jesus quotes this in John 15:25: "They hated me without reason."
"Zeal for your house consumes me." Quoted in John 2:17 when Jesus cleanses the temple. The psalm’s christological centre: the one consumed by zeal for God’s house.
"Rescue me from the mire; do not let the floodwaters engulf me. Answer me, LORD, out of the goodness of your love." The desperate plea: rescue from drowning, from the pit, from the deep.

refs.note: The disciples recall 'zeal for your house consumes me' when Jesus cleanses the temple.
Jesus cites 'they hated me without reason' as fulfilled in his rejection.
Paul applies 'the insults of those who insult you have fallen on me' to Christ bearing others' reproach.

hist.context: Psalm 69 is the second most-quoted psalm in the New Testament after Psalm 22. Attributed to David, it describes overwhelming suffering using flood imagery — sinking in mire, deep waters, the pit. The sufferer endures reproach for God's sake (v.7), becoming an outsider even to family. The reference to 'zeal for your house' (v.9) established this as a psalm of the righteous sufferer.

sections.header: Verses 19–36 — “They Put Gall in My Food and Gave Me Vinegar to Drink”

heb.paragraph: "They put gall in my food and gave me vinegar (ḥōmeṣ) for my thirst" (v.21). Fulfilled at the cross: "They offered Jesus wine mixed with gall... and vinegar on a sponge" (Matt 27:34, 48). The imprecatory section follows (vv.22–28): may their table become a snare (quoted in Rom 11:9–10). David ends with praise: "I will praise God’s name in song" (v.30). God will save Zion (v.35).

notes.note: "They put gall in my food and gave me vinegar for my thirst." Fulfilled at the crucifixion (Matt 27:34, 48; John 19:29). The detail is exact: food with gall, drink with vinegar.
"May their table become a snare." Paul quotes this in Romans 11:9–10 for Israel’s hardening. The imprecation becomes apostolic theology.
"I will praise God’s name in song. This will please the LORD more than an ox." The sacrifice-vs-praise contrast from Psalm 50: God prefers the song of a broken heart to the blood of a bull.
"God will save Zion. Those who love his name will dwell there." The national hope: Zion saved, the faithful settled. Personal suffering produces communal restoration.

refs.note: Jesus offered wine vinegar to drink fulfills 'they gave me vinegar for my thirst.'
Jesus's 'I thirst' and receiving vinegar completes the psalm's passion connection.
Peter applies 'may their place be deserted' to Judas, finding prophetic warrant in the psalm.

hist.context: The imprecatory section (vv.22-28) curses enemies with blindness, trembling, and blotting from the book of life. Early Christians read these curses as prophecy of Israel's rejection of Messiah. The shift to praise (vv.30-36) promises restoration of Zion and Judah's cities, suggesting post-exilic editing expanded the psalm's original scope.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: Sufferer in deep waters whose zeal for God's house consumes him (vv.1-4,7-12,29-36)
ppl.text: David is drowning: 'the waters have come up to my neck… I have come into the deep waters; the floods engulf me' (vv.1-2). His suffering is for God's sake: 'zeal for your house consumes me, and the insults of those who insult you fall on me' (v.9) — quoted by Jesus's disciples in John 2:17 during the temple cleansing. The psalm is the second most quoted in the NT after Psalm 22, with v.21 ('They put gall in my food and gave me vinegar for my thirst') fulfilled at the cross (Matt 27:34,48). David is mocked 'among those who sit at the gate' and is 'the song of the drunkards' (v.12) — public humiliation from civic leaders and tavern crowds alike.

ppl.name: God (Yahweh / Elohim)
ppl.role: The rescuer whose answer will cause creation to praise (vv.13-18,30-36)
ppl.text: David appeals to God's 'great love' and 'sure salvation' (v.13), asking rescue from the 'mire' and 'deep waters' (vv.14-15). God's answer will inspire cosmic worship: 'Let heaven and earth praise him, the seas and all that move in them' (v.34). The psalm pivots from individual suffering to national restoration: 'God will save Zion and rebuild the cities of Judah' (v.35). This trajectory — personal suffering leading to communal redemption — makes the psalm a theological template for the Passion narratives.

ppl.name: The Enemies
ppl.role: Those who hate David without reason and add to his suffering (vv.4-5,19-28)
ppl.text: David's enemies 'outnumber the hairs of my head' and 'are powerful' (v.4). He is isolated: 'I looked for sympathy, but there was none, for comforters, but I found none' (v.20). The imprecatory section (vv.22-28) asks God to 'let their table become a snare' and 'let their eyes be darkened' — Paul quotes this in Romans 11:9-10 regarding Israel's temporary hardening. These are among Scripture's most severe curses, reflecting the intensity of unjust suffering.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVFor I endure scorn for your sake, and shame covers my face.ESVFor it is for your sake that I have borne reproach, that dishonor has covered my face.NIVfor zeal for your house consumes me, and the insults of those who insult you fall on me.ESVFor zeal for your house has consumed me, and the reproaches of those who reproach you have fallen on me.

rec.text: ""Save me, O God, for the waters have come up to my neck. I sink in the miry depths. My throat is parched." Total submersion: neck-deep water, bottomless mud, dry throat. The imagery is of drowning in …"

rows.label: Gall and Vinegar — 69:19–36
rows.text: "Gall in my food, vinegar for my thirst" (Matt 27:34). Table becomes a snare (Rom 11:9). Praise over sacrifice.

hebtext.note: "Those who hate me (shiṭnâ) without reason outnumber the hairs of my head" (v.4). The second most-quoted messianic psalm after Psalm 22. The waters engulf David (v.1), he sinks in mire (v.2), his throat is parched (v.3). "Zeal for your house consumes me, and the insults of those who insult you fall on me" (v.9) — applied to Jesus cleansing the temple (John 2:17). David is mocked: sackcloth becomes a byword, drunkards make songs about him (vv.11–12).

"They put gall in my food and gave me vinegar (ḥōmeṣ) for my thirst" (v.21). Fulfilled at the cross: "They offered Jesus wine mixed with gall... and vinegar on a sponge" (Matt 27:34, 48). The imprecatory section follows (vv.22–28): may their table become a snare (quoted in Rom 11:9–10). David ends with praise: "I will praise God’s name in song" (v.30). God will save Zion (v.35).

themes.note: Prophecy peaks at 10: second most NT-quoted psalm. John 2:17 (temple zeal), John 15:25 (hated without reason), Matt 27:34/48 (gall and vinegar), Rom 11:9 (table as snare). Justice: imprecatory section.
